|  | | --- | --- | |  | | | Men's [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") | | | | [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") | | | | Bronze medal – third place | [1966 England](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup") | [Team](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup_squads#Portugal "1966 FIFA World Cup squads") | | [FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Confederations_Cup "FIFA Confederations Cup") | | | | Bronze medal – third place | [2017 Russia](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup") | [Team](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup_squads#Portugal "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup squads") | | [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") | | | | Gold medal – first place | [2016 France](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") | [Team](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_squads#Portugal "UEFA Euro 2016 squads") | | Silver medal – second place | [2004 Portugal](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004") | [Team](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004_squads#Portugal "UEFA Euro 2004 squads") | | [UEFA Nations League](/wiki/UEFA_Nations_League "UEFA Nations League") | | | | Gold medal – first place | [2019 Portugal](/wiki/2019_UEFA_Nations_League_Finals "2019 UEFA Nations League Finals") | [Team](/wiki/2019_UEFA_Nations_League_Finals_squads#Portugal "2019 UEFA Nations League Finals squads") | | Gold medal – first place | [2025 Germany](/wiki/2025_UEFA_Nations_League_Finals "2025 UEFA Nations League Finals") | [Team](/wiki/2025_UEFA_Nations_League_Finals_squads#Portugal "2025 UEFA Nations League Finals squads") | |

([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Seleção Portuguesa de Futebol*) has represented [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") competition since 1921. The national team is controlled by the [Portuguese Football Federation](/wiki/Portuguese_Football_Federation "Portuguese Football Federation") (FPF), the governing body for [football in Portugal](/wiki/Football_in_Portugal "Football in Portugal"). Portugal's home stadium is the [Estádio Nacional](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Nacional "Estádio Nacional") in [Oeiras](/wiki/Oeiras,_Portugal "Oeiras, Portugal"), located next to its primary [training ground](/wiki/Training_ground_(association_football) "Training ground (association football)") and the FPF headquarters (*[Cidade do Futebol](/wiki/Cidade_do_Futebol "Cidade do Futebol")*), but the team usually plays its home matches in more [modern stadiums](/wiki/List_of_football_stadiums_in_Portugal "List of football stadiums in Portugal") throughout the country. The head coach is [Roberto Martínez](/wiki/Roberto_Mart%C3%ADnez "Roberto Martínez"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and the captain is [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo"), who holds the team records for most caps and most goals.

The **Portugal national football team** ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Seleção Portuguesa de Futebol*) has represented [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") competition since 1921. The national team is controlled by the [Portuguese Football Federation](/wiki/Portuguese_Football_Federation "Portuguese Football Federation") (FPF), the governing body for [football in Portugal](/wiki/Football_in_Portugal "Football in Portugal"). Portugal's home stadium is the [Estádio Nacional](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Nacional "Estádio Nacional") in [Oeiras](/wiki/Oeiras,_Portugal "Oeiras, Portugal"), located next to its primary [training ground](/wiki/Training_ground_(association_football) "Training ground (association football)") and the FPF headquarters (*[Cidade do Futebol](/wiki/Cidade_do_Futebol "Cidade do Futebol")*), but the team usually plays its home matches in more [modern stadiums](/wiki/List_of_football_stadiums_in_Portugal "List of football stadiums in Portugal") throughout the country. The head coach is [Roberto Martínez](/wiki/Roberto_Mart%C3%ADnez "Roberto Martínez"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and the captain is [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo"), who holds the team records for most caps and most goals.

Portugal's first participation in a major tournament finals was at the [1966 World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"), which saw a team featuring [Ballon d'Or](/wiki/Ballon_d%27Or "Ballon d'Or") winner [Eusébio](/wiki/Eus%C3%A9bio "Eusébio") finish in third place. Portugal also made it to the semi-finals of the [UEFA Euro 1984](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1984 "UEFA Euro 1984"), losing to the hosts and eventual winners [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"). After missing out the [1998 World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"), Portugal qualified for the [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") and started an uninterrupted streak of qualification for every European Championship and World Cup finals. In this run, Portugal finished fourth at the [2006 World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup") and runners-up at the [Euro 2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"), which they entered as hosts, and reached the semi-finals of the [Euro 2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000") and [Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"). This was in great part due to the production of several world class players, such as Ballon d'Or winners [Luís Figo](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Figo "Luís Figo") and Cristiano Ronaldo.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)
In 2016, Portugal won its first-ever major trophy by defeating hosts France in the [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") [final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_final "UEFA Euro 2016 final"). With this win, the team qualified for and made its only appearance in the [FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Confederations_Cup "FIFA Confederations Cup"), [held in Russia](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup"), where they finished in third place. Portugal then qualified for and hosted the [first finals](/wiki/2019_UEFA_Nations_League_Finals "2019 UEFA Nations League Finals") of the [UEFA Nations League](/wiki/UEFA_Nations_League "UEFA Nations League") in [2019](/wiki/2019_UEFA_Nations_League "2019 UEFA Nations League"), where it defeated the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") to earn their second major title. Six years later, Portugal won its second Nations League and third overall title after defeating the [incumbent Nations League](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_UEFA_Nations_League "2022–23 UEFA Nations League") and [Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024") title holders [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") in the [2025 final](/wiki/2025_UEFA_Nations_League_final "2025 UEFA Nations League final") in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich").

Portugal is colloquially referred to as the *Seleção das [Quinas](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Portugal "Coat of arms of Portugal")* (a synecdoche based on the flag of the country) and has several notable rivalries with other national teams. These include [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team"), due to their shared history and close cultural ties;[[6]](#cite_note-6) Spain, due to their historical rivalry as Iberian neighbours;[[7]](#cite_note-Fanatics-7) and France, due to several high-stake meetings between both teams at the Euro and World Cup.[[8]](#cite_note-rivalidade-8)

## History

Main article: [History of the Portugal national football team](/wiki/History_of_the_Portugal_national_football_team "History of the Portugal national football team")

### Early World Cup attempts

Portugal were not invited to the [1930 World Cup](/wiki/1930_FIFA_World_Cup "1930 FIFA World Cup"), which only featured a finals stage and no [qualification round](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "FIFA World Cup qualification"). The team took part in the [1934 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1934_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1934 FIFA World Cup qualification"), but failed to eliminate their Spanish opponents, aggregating two defeats in the two-legged round, with a 9–0 loss in [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") and 2–1 loss in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") for an aggregate score of 11–1.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In the [1938 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1938 FIFA World Cup qualification"), the *Seleção* played one game against Switzerland held in neutral ground in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"). They lost 2–1 and failed to qualify for the finals.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") delayed the World Cup until [1950](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup "1950 FIFA World Cup") and subsequently, the national team rarely played.[[12]](#cite_note-12) A 10–0 home friendly loss against [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team"), two years after the war, still stands as their biggest ever defeat.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### 1950s and early 1960s

Similar to 1934, Portugal [were to play](/wiki/1950_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification#Group_6 "1950 FIFA World Cup qualification") a two-legged round against [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"). After a 5–1 defeat in Madrid, they managed to draw the second game 2–2. With a 7–3 aggregate score, they did not qualify on the pitch, however they would later be invited to replace [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team"), which had withdrawn from participating. Portugal refused to participate.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In [1954 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1954_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1954 FIFA World Cup qualification"), the team would play [Austria](/wiki/Austria_national_football_team "Austria national football team"); the Austrians won the first game with a 9–1 result.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The best the Portuguese could do was hold the Austrians to a goalless draw in Lisbon, resulting in a 9–1 aggregate defeat.[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Four years later](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_8 "1958 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 8"), Portugal won a qualifying match for the first time, a 3–0 home victory over [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"). Nevertheless, they finished last in a group that also featured [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team"); only the first-placed team, Northern Ireland, would qualify.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

1960 was the year that UEFA created the [European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship"). The [first edition](/wiki/1960_European_Nations%27_Cup "1960 European Nations' Cup") was a knock-out tournament with the last four teams participating in the finals stage that only featured one leg while the earlier stages had two legs. In the first round, the *Seleção das Quinas* won 2–0 at [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany_national_football_team "East Germany national football team") and then 3–2 in [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto"), advancing with a 5–2 two-legged win.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Portugal faced Yugoslavia in the quarter-finals, losing 6–3 on aggregate.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Portugal faced England and [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_national_football_team "Luxembourg national football team") in [1962 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1962_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_6 "1962 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 6") and ended up second in the group, behind England, who would be the only team in Group 6 to qualify.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In the [1964 European Championship](/wiki/1964_European_Nations%27_Cup "1964 European Nations' Cup"), Portugal played against [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_national_football_team "Bulgaria national football team") in the [qualifying rounds](/wiki/1964_European_Nations%27_Cup_qualifying "1964 European Nations' Cup qualifying"). The Portuguese lost in [Sofia](/wiki/Sofia "Sofia") and won in Lisbon. With the round tied 4–4, a replay was needed in a neutral country.[[23]](#cite_note-auto1-23) In the [Stadio Olimpico](/wiki/Stadio_Olimpico "Stadio Olimpico") in Rome, Portugal lost 1–0 thanks to a late strike from [Georgi Asparuhov](/wiki/Georgi_Asparuhov "Georgi Asparuhov").[[23]](#cite_note-auto1-23)

### Third place at the 1966 World Cup

Portugal were drawn with [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_national_football_team "Czechoslovakia national football team"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") and Turkey for [1966 World Cup qualification](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_4 "1966 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 4").[[24]](#cite_note-1966WC-24) They topped the group with only one draw and one defeat in six games and finally qualified for a FIFA World Cup, with a 1–0 away win against Czechoslovakia and Turkey and a 5–1 home win against the Turks being notable results.[[24]](#cite_note-1966WC-24)

At the World Cup, the team started out with three wins in the group stage after they beat [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team") 3–1,[[25]](#cite_note-25) Bulgaria 3–0, and two-time defending champions [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team") 3–1.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Secondly, they beat quarter-finalists [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea_national_football_team "North Korea national football team") 5–3, with [Eusébio](/wiki/Eus%C3%A9bio "Eusébio") getting four markers to overturn a 3–0 deficit.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Later, they reached the semi-finals where they were beaten by hosts England 2–1; in this game, Portugal would have played in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), but as England were the hosts, FIFA decided that the game would be played in London.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Portugal then defeated the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team") 2–1 in the third place match for their best World Cup finish to date.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Eusébio was the top scorer of the World Cup with nine goals. Portugal would not qualify for another World Cup for 20 years.

### 1980s

Portugal won their [Euro 1984 qualifying group](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1984_qualifying#Group_2 "UEFA Euro 1984 qualifying") that contained [Finland](/wiki/Finland_national_football_team "Finland national football team"), Poland and the Soviet Union with a win over the latter,[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) allowing them to qualify and be placed in Group B alongside Spain, [West Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") and Romania in the finals.[[32]](#cite_note-1984EC-32) In the first two matches, they drew 0–0 and 1–1 against West Germany and Spain, respectively.[[32]](#cite_note-1984EC-32) A 1–0 win over Romania resulted in a second-place finish in group play. Portugal were paired against hosts [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") in the semi-finals.[[32]](#cite_note-1984EC-32) After a draw in regular time, Portugal initially led 2–1 in extra time, but the hosts scored in the 114th and 119th minutes to eliminate the Portuguese 3–2 and go through to the final.[[32]](#cite_note-1984EC-32)

[For 1986 World Cup qualification](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA)#Group_2 "1986 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), the *Seleção* played against Czechoslovakia, [Malta](/wiki/Malta_national_football_team "Malta national football team"), Sweden and West Germany for the two spots that would guarantee them a ticket to Mexico.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Needing a win in the last game against West Germany in [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"), Portugal won the game to become the first team to beat West Germany at their home ground in an official match. The team exited early in the group stages after a win and two losses.[[34]](#cite_note-34) They started with a 1–0 win against England,[[35]](#cite_note-35) but later were beaten by Poland and [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco_national_football_team "Morocco national football team") 1–0 and 3–1 respectively.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Their staying in Mexico was marked by the [Saltillo Affair](/wiki/Saltillo_Affair "Saltillo Affair"), where players refused to train in order to win more prizes from the [Portuguese Football Federation](/wiki/Portuguese_Football_Federation "Portuguese Football Federation"). Mexico marked their last World Cup appearance until 2002.

### 1995–2006: The golden generation

At [UEFA Euro 1996](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996 "UEFA Euro 1996"), Portugal finished first in Group D, and in the [quarter-finals](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1996_knockout_stage#Quarter-finals "UEFA Euro 1996 knockout stage"), they lost 1–0 to the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Charisteas%27_Siegtreffer_im_Finale_der_Euro_2004.jpg/250px-Charisteas%27_Siegtreffer_im_Finale_der_Euro_2004.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charisteas%27_Siegtreffer_im_Finale_der_Euro_2004.jpg)

Portugal lost the [Euro 2004 final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004_final "UEFA Euro 2004 final") 0–1 to Greece with a header from [Angelos Charisteas](/wiki/Angelos_Charisteas "Angelos Charisteas") (pictured).

Portugal failed to [qualify](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA)#Group_9 "1998 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") for the [1998 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"). In [Euro 2000 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000_qualifying#Group_7 "UEFA Euro 2000 qualifying"), Portugal finished second in their group, one point short of first-placed Romania. However, after finishing as the top runner-up nation in qualifying, Portugal nonetheless secured a spot in the finals. They then defeated England 3–2, Romania 1–0 and Germany 3–0 to finish first in [Group A](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000_Group_A "UEFA Euro 2000 Group A"), then defeated Turkey in the quarter-finals. In the semi-finals against France, Portugal were eliminated in extra time when [Zinedine Zidane](/wiki/Zinedine_Zidane "Zinedine Zidane") converted a [penalty](/wiki/Penalty_kick_(association_football) "Penalty kick (association football)"). Referee [Günter Benkö](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Benk%C3%B6 "Günter Benkö") awarded the spot kick for a handball after [Abel Xavier](/wiki/Abel_Xavier "Abel Xavier") blocked a shot. Xavier, [Nuno Gomes](/wiki/Nuno_Gomes "Nuno Gomes") and [Paulo Bento](/wiki/Paulo_Bento "Paulo Bento") were all given lengthy suspensions for subsequently shoving the referee.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The final eventually finished 2–1.

During [2002 FIFA World Cup qualifying](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_2 "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 2"), Portugal won the group.[[39]](#cite_note-FIFA2WC-39) Several problems and poor judgement decisions occurred during the preparation and tournament itself and were widely reported in the Portuguese press,[[39]](#cite_note-FIFA2WC-39) including questionable managing choices and some amateurism, as well as lack of agreement on prizes.[[39]](#cite_note-FIFA2WC-39) Portugal entered the tournament as favourites to win Group D.[[39]](#cite_note-FIFA2WC-39) However, they were upset 3–2 by the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team").[[39]](#cite_note-FIFA2WC-39) They then rebounded with a 4–0 victory over Poland.[[39]](#cite_note-FIFA2WC-39) Needing a draw to advance, they lost the last group game to hosts [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team").[[40]](#cite_note-40) Portugal underachieved and ended third in its group stage, subsequently eliminated. Manager [António Oliveira](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Oliveira_(footballer,_born_1952) "António Oliveira (footballer, born 1952)") was fired after the World Cup.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Cristiano_Ronaldo_20120609.jpg/250px-Cristiano_Ronaldo_20120609.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cristiano_Ronaldo_20120609.jpg)

Ronaldo, pictured playing against [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") at [Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"), assumed the captaincy in the wake of Euro 2008.

The next major competition, the [UEFA Euro 2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"), was held in Portugal. For preparation, the Football Federation made a contract with [Luiz Felipe Scolari](/wiki/Luiz_Felipe_Scolari "Luiz Felipe Scolari") to manage the team until the tournament ended.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The host nation lost the first game against Greece 1–2.[[42]](#cite_note-42) They achieved their first win against [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team") 2–0 and also beat Spain 1–0.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) They went on to play against England, in a 2–2 draw that went into penalties, with Portugal winning.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Portugal beat the Netherlands 2–1 in the semi-finals,[[46]](#cite_note-46) and suffered a second defeat from Greece, 1–0, in the final.[[47]](#cite_note-Szreter-47)

After the tournament ended, many players belonging to the *Geração de Ouro* (Golden Generation), abandoned their international footballing careers, with only [Luís Figo](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Figo "Luís Figo") remaining in the team, despite a temporary retirement.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) The silver lining for Portugal was the emergence of Cristiano Ronaldo, who was selected in the UEFA Euro All-Star team.[[50]](#cite_note-50) While Portugal were playing in the competition, Scolari agreed in a new two-year deal with the Federation.

Portugal finished first in the [qualifying round for the 2006 World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_3 "2006 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 3"),[[51]](#cite_note-Ronaldo_profile-51) and topped Group D in the World Cup, with victories over [Angola](/wiki/Angola_national_football_team "Angola national football team") (1–0), [Iran](/wiki/Iran_national_football_team "Iran national football team") (2–0) and [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico_national_football_team "Mexico national football team") (2–1).[[52]](#cite_note-Por._Vs_Iran-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)
Portugal defeated the Netherlands 1–0 in the Round of 16 in an infamous matched that has come to be known as the [Battle of Nuremberg](/wiki/Battle_of_Nuremberg_(2006_FIFA_World_Cup) "Battle of Nuremberg (2006 FIFA World Cup)"), marked by violent fouls and many players [cautioned](/wiki/Penalty_card "Penalty card") or [sent off](/wiki/Ejection_(sports) "Ejection (sports)") by referee [Valentin Ivanov](/wiki/Valentin_Ivanov_(footballer,_born_1961) "Valentin Ivanov (footballer, born 1961)").[[54]](#cite_note-54) Portugal drew 0–0 after extra time with England in the quarter-final, but won 3–1 on [penalties](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)") to reach their first World Cup semi-final since 1966.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) Portugal lost the semi-final 1–0 against France,[[57]](#cite_note-57) and were then defeated 3–1 by the tournament hosts, Germany, in the third-place play-off match.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### 2006–2014: Post-golden generation and mixed results

For [Euro 2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008") Portugal finished second in [qualification](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying_Group_A "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying Group A") behind Poland,[[59]](#cite_note-59) and won their first two group games against Turkey and the Czech Republic, although a loss to co-hosts Switzerland set up a quarter-final matchup with Germany which the team lost 3–2.[[60]](#cite_note-60) After the tournament, Scolari left to take over at [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.").[[61]](#cite_note-61) Afterwards, [Carlos Queiroz](/wiki/Carlos_Queiroz "Carlos Queiroz") was appointed as the head coach of the Portugal national team.[[62]](#cite_note-CQ_Guardian-62)[[63]](#cite_note-CQ_Reuters_UK-63)[[64]](#cite_note-CQ_BBC_Sport-64)[[65]](#cite_note-CQ_AP-65)

Portugal [came second in the qualifying stages](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_1 "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 1") for the [2010 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup") under Queiroz, then beat [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina_national_football_team "Bosnia and Herzegovina national football team") in a [play-off](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_second_round "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round"), thereby reaching every tournament in the decade.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) A 19-match undefeated streak, in which the team conceded only three goals, ended with a loss to eventual champions Spain in the round of 16, 1–0.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Queiroz was later criticised for setting up his team in an overly cautious way.[[70]](#cite_note-70) After the World Cup, squad regulars [Simão](/wiki/Sim%C3%A3o_Sabrosa "Simão Sabrosa"), [Paulo Ferreira](/wiki/Paulo_Ferreira "Paulo Ferreira"), [Miguel](/wiki/Miguel_Monteiro "Miguel Monteiro") and [Tiago](/wiki/Tiago_Mendes "Tiago Mendes") all retired from international football.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) Queiroz was banned from coaching the national team for one month after he tried to block a doping test to the team while preparing for the World Cup, as well as directing insulting words to the testers.[[74]](#cite_note-74) In consequence, he received a further six-month suspension. Several media outbursts from Queiroz[[75]](#cite_note-75) against the heads of the Portuguese Football Federation followed, which partly prompted his dismissal. Paulo Bento was appointed as his replacement at head coach.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Bento's team qualified for Euro 2012; they were drawn with Germany, Denmark, and the Netherlands in a widely speculated "[group of death](/wiki/Group_of_death "Group of death")".[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) They lost their first game 0–1 to Germany, then beat Denmark 3–2.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) The final group stage match was against the Netherlands. After [Van der Vaart](/wiki/Rafael_van_der_Vaart "Rafael van der Vaart") had given the Dutch a 1–0 lead, Ronaldo netted twice to ensure a 2–1 victory.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85) Portugal finished second in the group and qualified for the knockout phase. Portugal defeated the Czech Republic 1–0 in the quarter-finals with a header from Ronaldo.[[86]](#cite_note-86) The semi-final match was against Spain, who defeated Portugal 4–2 on penalties after a goalless draw.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In [2014 FIFA World Cup qualifying](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), Portugal won 4–2 on aggregate in a play-off against Sweden with all four goals being scored by Ronaldo, and were drawn into [Group G](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_Group_G "2014 FIFA World Cup Group G") with the United States, Germany and [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana_national_football_team "Ghana national football team"). Their first match against the Germans was their worst-ever defeat in a World Cup, a 4–0 loss.[[88]](#cite_note-88) They went on to draw 2–2 against the United States and won 2–1 against Ghana.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-BBC14-90) However, the team were eliminated due to inferior goal difference to the Americans.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### 2016–present: Euro 2016 and first international glories

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg/250px-Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Soccer_Field_Transparant.svg)

Portugal's starting lineup for the [UEFA Euro 2016 final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_final "UEFA Euro 2016 final")

In [UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying"), Bento was dismissed following a defeat to Albania and was replaced by [Fernando Santos](/wiki/Fernando_Santos_(footballer,_born_1954) "Fernando Santos (footballer, born 1954)") in September 2014.[[92]](#cite_note-92) After qualifying for the finals, Portugal finished third in Group F but advanced to the knockout stages as the third-best third place team following three straight draws. Portugal beat Croatia 1–0 after extra time in the round of 16[[93]](#cite_note-93) and then defeated Poland 5–3 on penalties to reach the semi-finals,[[94]](#cite_note-94) where they defeated [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") 2–0.[[95]](#cite_note-95) In the [final](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_final "UEFA Euro 2016 final") against the hosts, France, Ronaldo went off injured. In extra time, substitute [Eder](/wiki/Eder_(footballer,_born_1987) "Eder (footballer, born 1987)") scored the winning goal for Portugal in the 109th minute with a strike from 25 yards past [Hugo Lloris](/wiki/Hugo_Lloris "Hugo Lloris").[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

Following their [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") victory, Portugal participated in the [2017 FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup"). They finished top of their group,[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) but lost to [Chile](/wiki/Chile_national_football_team "Chile national football team") on penalties after a goalless draw in the [semi-finals](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup#Semi-finals "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup"),[[100]](#cite_note-100) but rebounded in the [third place game](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup#Third_place_play-off "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup"), defeating Mexico 2–1 after extra time.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Portugal_national_football_team_World_Cup_2018.jpg/250px-Portugal_national_football_team_World_Cup_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portugal_national_football_team_World_Cup_2018.jpg)

Portugal lining up before a match at the [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup")

At the [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup"), Portugal opened their campaign with a 3–3 draw with Spain, with Cristiano Ronaldo scoring a [hat-trick](/wiki/List_of_FIFA_World_Cup_hat-tricks "List of FIFA World Cup hat-tricks").[[102]](#cite_note-cbssports.com-102) After a 1–0 victory against Morocco,[[103]](#cite_note-103) Portugal drew 1–1 with Iran to progress to the knockout round as group runners-up.[[104]](#cite_note-104) Portugal were eliminated following a 2–1 defeat to [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team") in the [round of 16](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_knockout_stage "2018 FIFA World Cup knockout stage").[[105]](#cite_note-105)

Following the World Cup, Portugal won the inaugural [UEFA Nations League](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Nations_League "2018–19 UEFA Nations League") beating the Netherlands at the [Estádio do Dragão](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_do_Drag%C3%A3o "Estádio do Dragão") in Porto, with the only goal scored by [Gonçalo Guedes](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alo_Guedes "Gonçalo Guedes") in the 60th minute.[[106]](#cite_note-Sports_Mole-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

At [UEFA Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020"), Portugal were drawn into a group containing [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team") and [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team") which was widely speculated as being the "group of death".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Portugal advanced to the [next round](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020_knockout_stage "UEFA Euro 2020 knockout stage") by defeating Hungary, drawing with France and losing to Germany. There, they faced [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team") but lost 1–0.

For the [2022 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_A "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group A"), Portugal were required to qualify for the finals via the [play-offs](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_second_round "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA second round") after finishing second in their group.[[108]](#cite_note-108) Nevertheless, Portugal managed to beat [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team")[[109]](#cite_note-109) and [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia_national_football_team "North Macedonia national football team") to qualify for the final tournament.[[110]](#cite_note-110) At the 2022 World Cup, Portugal defeated [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana_national_football_team "Ghana national football team") 3–2 in their first group game[[111]](#cite_note-111) and then beat Uruguay 2–0.[[112]](#cite_note-112) to qualify for the knockout stages.[[113]](#cite_note-113) The Portuguese would demolish [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") 6–1 in the next round, their highest tally in a World Cup knockout game since the [1966 World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"), with [Gonçalo Ramos](/wiki/Gon%C3%A7alo_Ramos "Gonçalo Ramos") scoring a hat-trick.[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115) However, they were eliminated by tournament in the quarter-finals by [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco_national_football_team "Morocco national football team"), 1–0.[[116]](#cite_note-116) Following a disappointing World Cup campaign, Fernando Santos was dismissed on 15 December.[[117]](#cite_note-117) On 9 January 2023, [Roberto Martinez](/wiki/Roberto_Mart%C3%ADnez "Roberto Martínez") was announced as the new head coach of Portugal.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

## Team image

### Kits

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/40px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg)

Wikimedia Commons has media related to [Portugal national football team kits](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team_kits "commons:Portugal national football team kits").

Portugal's traditional [home kit](/wiki/Kit_(association_football) "Kit (association football)") is mainly red with a green trim, reflecting the colors of the nation's [flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Portugal "Flag of Portugal"). Over the years, the particular shade of red has alternated between a darker [burgundy](/wiki/Burgundy_(color) "Burgundy (color)") and a lighter [scarlet](/wiki/Scarlet_(color) "Scarlet (color)"). Both green and red shorts have been used to complete the strip.

The team's away kits, on the other hand, have varied more considerably. White has typically been preferred as a dominant color, either with blue shorts, or red and green highlights. In recent times, all-black has been utilised, as has a turquoise-teal color, the latter of which was prominently featured during the title winning [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") campaign.

### Goal records

Most goals scored in one World Cup
:   9 – [Eusébio](/wiki/Eus%C3%A9bio "Eusébio") ([1966](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"))[[122]](#cite_note-66WC-122)

Most goals scored in World Cup
:   9 – [Eusébio](/wiki/Eus%C3%A9bio "Eusébio") ([1966](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"))[[122]](#cite_note-66WC-122)

Most goals scored in one European Championship
:   5 – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") ([2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020"))

Most goals scored in European Championship
:   14 – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") ([2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"), [2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008"), [2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"), [2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") and [2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020"))[[123]](#cite_note-123)

Oldest goalscorer
:   40 years, 8 months and 9 days – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") (2–2 against [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_national_football_team "Hungary national football team") on 14 October 2025)

Youngest goalscorer
:   17 years, 9 months and 25 days – [Fernando Chalana](/wiki/Fernando_Chalana "Fernando Chalana") (2–1 against [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_national_football_team "Cyprus national football team") on 5 December 1976)

Most hat-tricks
:   10 – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") (includes four goals against [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra_national_football_team "Andorra national football team") on 7 October 2016 and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_national_football_team "Lithuania national football team") on 10 September 2019)[[124]](#cite_note-124)

Most pokers
:   2 – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo")

Youngest player to score a hat-trick
:   20 years, 11 months and 4 days – [André Silva](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Silva_(footballer,_born_1995) "André Silva (footballer, born 1995)") (6–0 against [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands_national_football_team "Faroe Islands national football team") on 10 October 2016)[[125]](#cite_note-125)

### Other records

Most matches played in World Cup
:   22 – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") ([2006](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup"), [2010](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup"), [2014](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup"), [2018](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup") and [2022](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup "2022 FIFA World Cup"))[[102]](#cite_note-cbssports.com-102)

Most matches played in European Championship
:   30 – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") ([2004](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004 "UEFA Euro 2004"), [2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008"), [2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"), [2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), [2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020") and [2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024"))[[126]](#cite_note-126)

Oldest player (outfield or goalkeeper)
:   41 years, 4 months and 9 days – [Pepe](/wiki/Pepe_(footballer,_born_1983) "Pepe (footballer, born 1983)") (0–0 against [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") on 5 July 2024)

Longest national career (outfield or goalkeeper)
:   22 years, 2 months and 25 days  – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") (from 20 August 2003 to 13 November 2025)

Youngest debutant
:   17 years, 6 months and 24 days – [Paulo Futre](/wiki/Paulo_Futre "Paulo Futre") (5–0 against [Finland](/wiki/Finland_national_football_team "Finland national football team") on 21 September 1983)[[127]](#cite_note-127)

Youngest player to reach 100 caps
:   27 years, 8 months and 11 days – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") (1–1 against [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") on 16 October 2012)[[128]](#cite_note-128)

Youngest player to reach 200 caps
:   38 years, 4 months and 15 days – [Cristiano Ronaldo](/wiki/Cristiano_Ronaldo "Cristiano Ronaldo") (1–0 against [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team") on 20 June 2023)
